messages.content: You are the planning module of a patent-drafting agent. Given an invention disclosure as JSON, reply with a short JSON plan of drafting steps. Reply with JSON only.

{"id":"uspto-19014047","title":"MULTI-AGENT SYSTEM IMPLEMENTING FEDERATED LEARNING FOR CYBERSECURITY APPLICATIONS","inventors":["Kubashen Jerome Naidoo"],"technology_class":"G06F","summary":"A multi-agent system implementing federated learning for cybersecurity applications. The system includes one or more processing devices that perform operations including receiving, from each node of multiple nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine-learning model. The corresponding machine learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node. Based on the parameters received from the multiple nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine-learning models. The global model parameters are transmitted to at least a subset of the multiple nodes of the distributed system, and the global model parameters are configured to update local model parameters of the corresponding machine learning model at each node.



ABST
19014047
1
3
2025-01-08

Attorney Docket No. 59103-0005001
ABSTRACT
A multi-agent system implementing federated learning for cybersecurity applications. The system includes one or more processing devices that perform operations including receiving, from each node of multiple nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine-learning model. The corresponding machine learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node. Based on the parameters received from the multiple nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine-learning models. The global model parameters are transmitted to at least a subset of the multiple nodes of the distributed system, and the global model parameters are configured to update local model parameters of the corresponding machine learning model at each node.

1
1. (Currently Amended) A system for implementing coordinated cybersecurity threat detection across a distributed system, the system comprising:a non-transitory storage device configured to store computer-readable instructions; and
one or more processing devices configured to execute the computer-readable instructions, which when executed cause the one or more processing devices to perform operations comprising:
receiving, from each node of multiple nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine-learning model that is trained at the corresponding node of the distributed system, wherein:
the corresponding machine learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node based on local data received by the corresponding node; and
the model updates are learned from the local data,
generating, based on the parameters received from the multiple nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine-learning models trained at the multiple nodes of the distributed system;
 and
transmitting to each node of at least a subset of the multiple nodes of the distributed system, a respective selected subset of the global model SVG

You are a patent attorney. Draft numbered patent claims (independent claim 1, then dependent claims) followed by a specification section for the disclosed invention. Close common loophole patterns: antecedent-basis gaps, means-plus-function overbreadth, missing dependent-claim fallbacks.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Loophole guardrails retrieved from memory for this technology class — close each one with explicit claim language; if none are listed, draft with standard care:
(none on record)

Plan:
{
  "drafting_steps": [
    "Extract core technical elements: multi-agent federated learning, distributed nodes, model updates, global parameters, context-based subset selection, cybersecurity threat detection, multi-modal data, weighted combination, TEE security, derivatives/loss functions.",
    "Map each claim amendment to corresponding independent claim language (system, method, storage media) and ensure consistent terminology.",
    "Identify key inventive concepts requiring explicit support: context-based subset selection, weighted combination based on node data characteristics, TEE-secured training data, multi-modal inputs (text/images/behavioral/biometrics), derivatives/loss function parameters.",
    "Structure independent claims to cover: (1) system architecture with processing devices/storage, (2) reception of model updates from nodes, (3) generation of global parameters via weighted combination, (4) transmission of context-selected subset, (5) update of local models, (6) supporting technical specifics (multi-modal data, TEE, derivatives).",
    "Draft dependent claims to add specificity: multi-modal data types, weighting criteria, TEE implementation, parameter type (derivatives/subset), and storage media encoding instructions.",
    "Verify claim scope aligns with disclosure: avoid overbroad language (e.g., "all global model parameters" vs. "selected subset"), ensure "context of the node" is consistently defined, and maintain technical precision for cybersecurity application context."
  ]
}

Disclosure:

transcript.reply:
**PATENT CLAIMS**

1.  A system for implementing coordinated cybersecurity threat detection across a distributed system, the system comprising:  
    a non-transitory storage device configured to store computer-readable instructions; and  
    one or more processing devices configured to execute the computer-readable instructions, which when executed cause the one or more processing devices to perform operations comprising:  
    receiving, from each node of a plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine‑learning model that is trained at the corresponding node of the distributed system, wherein:  
    the corresponding machine‑learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node based on local data received by the corresponding node; and  
    the model updates are learned from the local data;  
    generating, based on the parameters received from the plurality of nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine‑learning models trained at the plurality of nodes of the distributed system;  
    transmitting to each node of at least a subset of the plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a respective selected subset of the global model parameters, wherein:  
    the size of the respective selected subset is smaller than the size of all global model parameters; and  
    the respective selected subset is identified based at least on the context of the node; and  
    updating, at each node of the subset, local model parameters of the corresponding machine‑learning model using the global model parameters of the respective selected subset.

2.  The system of claim 1, wherein the corresponding machine‑learning model on a node of the plurality of nodes is trained to detect cybersecurity threats based on multi‑modal data that includes data of a first modality and at least a second modality different from the first modality.

3.  The system of claim 2, wherein each of the first modality and the second modality comprises one of: text, images, behavioral data, or biometric data.

4.  The system of claim 1, wherein generating the global model parameters comprises combining the model updates from individual nodes in a weighted combination.

5.  The system of claim 4, wherein a weight for a particular model update in the weighted combination is determined based on a characteristic of data used in training the machine‑learning model at the corresponding node.

6.  The system of claim 1, wherein data used in training the corresponding machine‑learning model at the corresponding node of the distributed system is secured within a trusted execution environment (TEE) at the corresponding node.

7.  The system of claim 1, wherein the plurality of parameters representative of the model updates comprises derivatives of one or more loss functions associated with the model parameters.

8.  The system of claim 1, wherein the plurality of parameters representative of the model updates comprises a subset of all model updates for the corresponding machine‑learning model.

9.  A method for implementing coordinated cybersecurity threat detection across a distributed system, the method comprising:  
    receiving, from each node of a plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine‑learning model that is trained at the corresponding node of the distributed system, wherein:  
    the corresponding machine‑learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node based on local data received by the corresponding node; and  
    the model updates are learned from the local data;  
    generating, based on the parameters received from the plurality of nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine‑learning models trained at the plurality of nodes of the distributed system;  
    transmitting to each node of at least a subset of the plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a respective selected subset of the global model parameters, wherein:  
    the size of the respective selected subset is smaller than the size of all global model parameters; and  
    the respective selected subset is identified based at least on the context of the node; and  
    updating, at each node of the subset, local model parameters of the corresponding machine‑learning model using the global model parameters of the respective selected subset.

10.  The method of claim 9, wherein the corresponding machine‑learning model on a node of the plurality of nodes is trained to detect cybersecurity threats based on multi‑modal data that includes data of a first modality and at least a second modality different from the first modality.

11.  The method of claim 10, wherein each of the first modality and the second modality comprises one of: text, images, behavioral data, or biometric data.

12.  The method of claim 9, wherein generating the global model parameters comprises combining the model updates from individual nodes in a weighted combination; and a weight for a particular model update in the weighted combination is determined based on a characteristic of data used in training the machine‑learning model at the corresponding node.

13.  The method of claim 9, wherein data used in training the corresponding machine‑learning model at the corresponding node of the distributed system is secured within a trusted execution environment (TEE) at the corresponding node.

14.  The method of claim 9, wherein the plurality of parameters representative of the model updates comprises one

messages.content:
You are a hostile patent examiner. Attack the draft below: find §112 indefiniteness, antecedent-basis errors, §101/§102/§103 exposure, and claim language a competitor could design around. Ground every defect in the standards below and cite the MPEP section. Reply as a bulleted list of defects, most severe first.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Known loophole patterns retrieved from memory — check each explicitly; if none are listed, apply standard scrutiny:
(none on record)

**PATENT CLAIMS**

1.  A system for implementing coordinated cybersecurity threat detection across a distributed system, the system comprising:  
    a non-transitory storage device configured to store computer-readable instructions; and  
    one or more processing devices configured to execute the computer-readable instructions, which when executed cause the one or more processing devices to perform operations comprising:  
    receiving, from each node of a plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine‑learning model that is trained at the corresponding node of the distributed system, wherein:  
    the corresponding machine‑learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node based on local data received by the corresponding node; and  
    the model updates are learned from the local data;

transcript.reply:
- **§112(b) indefiniteness – “selected subset” lacks antecedent basis and standard of size**  
  The claim recites “a respective selected subset of the global model parameters, wherein the size of the respective selected subset is smaller than the size of all global model parameters” without defining what “selected subset” refers to or how “smaller” is measured; under *MPEP §2173* this renders the claim indefinite because the scope is not reasonably certain and the term “selected” has no antecedent in the specification.

- **§112(b) indefiniteness – “based at least on the context of the node” is vague and lacks a benchmark**  
  The phrase “identified based at least on the context of the node” is a term of degree with no disclosed metric or threshold; per *MPEP §2173* it fails to provide a definite standard for determining inclusion, making the claim uncertain.

- **§112(a) written description – No support for “selected subset identified based at least on the context of the node”**  
  The specification does not disclose any concrete method or criteria for selecting a subset based on node context; under *MPEP §2163* the claim exceeds what is possessed, creating indefiniteness and lack of written description.

- **§112(a) enablement – No algorithmic or structural disclosure for how the subset is selected or how the weighted combination is performed**  
  The claim requires execution of a context‑based selection and weighted combination without providing the corresponding algorithm or data structures; per *MPEP §2164* a skilled artisan cannot practice the invention without undue experimentation.

- **§101 subject‑matter eligibility – Abstract idea of “coordinated cybersecurity threat detection” performed on a generic computer**  
  The claim is directed to the abstract concept of aggregating model updates for threat detection; it does not recite a specific technical improvement to computer operation (e.g., a novel data structure, algorithm, or hardware‑level optimization) and therefore fails the *MPEP §2106* two‑step test (Prongs 1–2) as an abstract idea implemented on a generic system.

- **§102 anticipation – Prior‑art reference discloses a system that receives model‑update parameters, generates global parameters, and transmits a subset thereof to nodes**  
  A single prior‑art system (e.g., federated learning framework such as TensorFlow Federated) teaches receiving local model updates, aggregating them into global parameters, and sending a reduced‑size subset to selected nodes, satisfying every limitation of claim 1; thus claim 1 is anticipated under *MPEP §2131*.

- **§103 obviousness – Combination of known federated‑learning and weighted‑aggregation techniques renders claim 1 obvious**  
  Known prior art teaches both federated model aggregation and weighting updates by data‑characteristics; a person of ordinary skill would readily combine these teachings to obtain a smaller subset based on node context, yielding predictable results; no unexpected technical effect is shown, making claim 1 obvious under *MPEP §2141‑2143*.

- **§102 anticipation – Method claim 9 is anticipated by the same prior‑art system performing the identical steps of receiving updates, generating global parameters, transmitting a subset, and updating local models**  
  The prior‑art reference also discloses the method steps enumerated in claim 9; therefore claim 9 is directly anticipated under *MPEP §2131*.

- **§112(f) means‑plus‑function – “global model parameters” and “selected subset” function as “means for” without structural disclosure**  
  The claim recites functional language (“generating… a set of global model parameters… transmitting… a respective selected subset… updating…”) that qualifies as §112(f) means‑plus‑function; because the specification does not disclose the corresponding structure (e.g., specific aggregation algorithm, data structures, or hardware modules), the claim is indefinite under *MPEP §2181*.

- **§112(b) indefiniteness – “weight for a particular model update… determined based on a characteristic of data” lacks a concrete criterion**  
  The weight‑determination step uses an undefined “characteristic of data” without a benchmark; per *MPEP §2173* this renders the claim indefinite as the scope of the weight calculation is not reasonably certain.

messages.content:
You are a patent attorney revising your own draft after a hostile examiner's critique. Apply every material defect below against the standards: fix §112 indefiniteness and antecedent-basis gaps, narrow means-plus-function overbreadth, add dependent-claim fallbacks, resolve §101/§102/§103 exposure, and foreclose each design-around named. Output the COMPLETE corrected claim set (independent claim 1, then dependents) followed by the specification — same format as the original, not a diff.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

**PATENT CLAIMS**

1.  A system for implementing coordinated cybersecurity threat detection across a distributed system, the system comprising:  
    a non‑transitory storage device configured to store computer‑readable instructions; and  
    one or more processing devices configured to execute the computer‑readable instructions, which when executed cause the one or more processing devices to perform operations comprising:  
    receiving, from each node of a plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a plurality of parameters representative of model updates to a corresponding machine‑learning model that is trained at the corresponding node of the distributed system, wherein:  
    the corresponding machine‑learning model is trained to detect cybersecurity threats within a context of the corresponding node based on local data received by the corresponding node; and  
    the model updates are learned from the local data;  
    generating, based on the parameters received from the plurality of nodes, a set of global model parameters that represents global updates to the individual machine‑learning models trained at the plurality of nodes of the distributed system;  
    transmitting to each node of at least a subset of the plurality of nodes of the distributed system, a respective selected subset of the global model parameters, wherein:  
    the size of the respective selected subset is smaller than the size of all global model parameters; and  
    the respective selected subset is identified based at least on a node‑specific context metric that quantifies the node’s operational context; and  
    updating, at each node of the subset, local model parameters of the corresponding machine‑learning model using the global model parameters of the respective selected subset.

2.  The system of claim 1, wherein the corresponding machine‑learning model on a node of the plurality of nodes is trained to detect cybersecurity threats based on multi‑modal data that includes data of a first modality and at least a second modality different from the first modality.
